text: the time. Each of the four archetypes is the embodiment of organizing principles in the universe, described in animistic fashion. Each one of the directions represents one of the steps that the shaman must go through to become a man or woman of knowledge. The shaman differentiates between information and knowledge. Information is what we are flooded in every day. Knowledge is wisdom. Information is knowing that water is two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. Knowledge is being able to make it rain. The shaman is a person of the percept, of perceptual traditions. In the West, we are people of the precept . We get precepts, laws, rules. We get the Ten Commandments, elect legislators and lawmakers who make more rules. When the shamans want to change the world, they work at the level of the essential to bring about a shift in perception. By shifting perception, we dream the new world into being and the world changes. That is our task, to dream with our eyes open. Through this great transformation, a new human is emerging on the earth. I call this new human Homo luminous . In the West, we believe evolution happens in between generations: maybe your children will be smarter and more handsome, maybe the indigo children will climb to the next rung on the evolutionary ladder. The shaman understands that evolution happens within generations. It is for us to take that quantum leap into who we are becoming. We can become Homo luminous in our lifetime. This is our greatest task: to take that quantum leap individually, because as we do it for ourselves, we do it for the entire planet. Each and every one of us, when we choose truth, when we choose life; when we choose life, we are transforming the world. 21 JOURNEY THROUGH A COSMIC PORTAL: A SERPENT LADDER FRANKLIN LAVOIE The death and mourning of a beloved friend opened a cosmic portal, which gave rise to hypnogogic trance, wherein I encountered a “serpent ladder.” In May 2005 I was in Tulum, Mexico, for a memorial service to honor the life of Sanna Parker, a community leader, a free-spirit dancer, and a charismatic friend. Some family and friends gathered in Tulum to celebrate Sanna’s life and memory. We participated in the ceremony called “Lifting the Shadow.” I arrived at the Buffalo Airport, May 18, 2005, at around 5:30 a.m. Turns out, I didn’t know my itinerary: no departure time, flight number, connecting city, not even what airline I was flying on. To make matters worse, I had to enter all this information into a computer terminal before I could even approach somebody for assistance. The pressure of not knowing if I was late, coupled with feeling idiotic, plus the computer protocol, all caused emotional turmoil. I felt like the Fool from the Tarot deck (card number 0)—not only did I feel foolish, but my knowledge of my itinerary was zero. But I was steeped in meditation practice, so I observed all this and forgave myself. Under the circumstances, what’s the worst that could happen? I’d have to stay home, and I could live with that. It was a stressful situation for fifteen minutes, until my friends arrived. We got our tickets and were on our way. I’d cut myself some slack and maintained my composure in a stressful situation. I’d passed some kind of emotional trial on the threshold of a cosmic portal. We got to Tulum that afternoon, and the next evening we participated in the first part of the Lifting the Shadow ritual. This timeless ceremony was exquisitely performed by two mystical friends of Sanna’s from Tulum. I appreciated every word and gesture they made. Thirteen candles were lit ceremonially around on a simple cross, bound with flowers, bound with prayers. Three days later we would finish the ceremony: dismantle the altar, take the flowery cross to the sea, and offer prayers on Sanna’s behalf, thanking the universe for her fifty-two-year sojourn with us. Meanwhile, the day after the first part of the ritual, we headed to Playa Paraíso, or Paradise Beach—I and seven of Sanna’s family and friends (all women, except me). I put on a mask, snorkel, and fins and headed for the water. When up to my crotch in water, two things happened simultaneously: four large fish nearly brushed my knees and a voice just like thunder clearly spoke my name. In an instant I dismissed the voice as some sort of auditory hallucination. “Maybe a dump truck grinding its motor,” I thought, assuming my auditory nerves had dredged up my name from memory somehow. But the fish, on the other hand, were plainly real, so I went swimming without turning around. Some minutes later I discovered that I was swimming with my fanny pack on. It contained my wallet, my fancy new camera, my money, and ID, plus my papers for my return to the States. How do you say “crap!” in Mayan? Disturbing as it was, I instantly chose to lighten up on myself. I didn’t piss ‘n’ moan to make matters worse. I quipped that I was “just having a little trouble in Paradise.” I spared myself a lot of grief while my wet items dried. It hadn’t escaped my attention that I was at Playa Paraíso, in “paradise,” and the sun was virtually on the zenith, conjunct the Seven Sisters, and here I was in ceremony with seven women. Meanwhile, I’d just emerged from the sea with my ID “all washed up.” What if I’d turned to identify that thunderous voice? It was like the voice of Chacmool—the local Mayan Rain Spirit. This probably wouldn’t have happened. As it was, I’d negotiated another emotional ordeal, with a meditative heart, drawing nearer to the cosmic portal. My decision to be kind, forgive myself, and lighten up (despite the circumstances) feels paramount to what happened next.
